item.title: 01 Classes and Objects
item.description: Key Concepts object, class, instance, method, signature, parameter, type, state, source code, return value, compiler Java objects model objects from a problem domain. Objects are created from classes. The class describes the kind of object; the objects represent individual instances of the class We can communicate with objects by invoking methods on them. Objects usually do something if we invoke a method. Methods can have parameters to provide additional information for a task. The method name and the parameter types of a method are called its signature.

item.title: Data definitions
item.description: What is a information Anything you can sense is information. music colors the name of school Basically information is stuff in the real world. You cant literally put this kind of information into computer. 01001010100010 name of an mp3 number number of kids in your school ip adress We can represent things in the real world in a form the computer can understand. Shoe Brand, Price, Qty, Color, ID, Model, Location using the innformation we would like to answer some questions check if a shoe is in stock check what is the most expensive shoe which store is in stock Atomic This is when information can be captured by a single atomic value

item.title: How to design functions
item.description: What is a design recipe? Creating a function involves a series of several steps The design recipe is a systematic approach to designing functions. Steps in the design recipe Create the contract and stub Contract Every function will take some arguments. Those arguments have some type. When you make a function one of the first things to do is to state what types of input can this function handle. Additionally, every function will output a value, and that value has a type.

item.title: 03 Object interactions
item.description: Key Ideas Abstraction is the ability to ignore details of parts to focus attention on a higher level of a problem. Modularization is the process of dividing a whole into well-defined parts, which can be built and examined separately, and which interact in well-defined ways. The class diagram shows the classes of an applica-tion and the relationships between them. It gives infor-mation about the source code and presents the static view of a program.

item.title: Chapter 1 What does the CPU do?
item.description: Key Ideas Computers can process any type of information Information in the real world comes in many forms. A school bell is information. It tells us when class starts or ends. Light from the sun coming through the window tells us its day time. Your calling your name from across the room tells you your friend wants your attention.

item.title: Pedagogical Languages
item.description: A beginners main concern is to develop problem solving skills that allow them to design high quality programs. While one can learn to program with an industry level language like Python or C++, it should be noted that these languages were not designed with the beginner in mind and hence make the process of developing problem solving skills more difficult. Learning to design programs is primarily about the study of principles and the acquisition of transferable skills.

item.title: My Teaching Principles
item.description: Excellent programmers are excellent problem solvers. Thus, helping students become problem solvers is one of my top priorities. Getting comfortable reasoning abstractly and learning how to manage the complexity inherit in writing large programs are two of the greatest challenges new programmers face. It is my view that anyone is capable of solving difficult problems provided they develop habits. The specific behaviors needed to excel in a given field will vary. Though, in general, efficient practice over time leads to mastery.
